original_abstract: Capturing and recycling $CO_2$ into valuable chemicals like methanol is a promising strategy for mitigating greenhouse gas emissions. However, the thermodynamic stability of $CO_2$ makes its activation a significant kinetic bottleneck. While bulk gold is catalytically inert for hydrogenation, it can be transformed into a highly active catalyst when dispersed as nanoparticles on specific oxide supports. In this study, we combine experimental measurements and density functional theory (DFT) to demonstrate that gold nanoparticles anchored on a $CeO_x/TiO_2$ substrate exhibit exceptional activity for $CO_2$ conversion. We find that electronic polarization at the metal-oxide interface creates unique active centers that facilitate $CO_2$ adsorption and subsequent hydrogenation at unexpectedly low pressures. This synergistic effect not only boosts overall reaction rates but also significantly shifts selectivity toward methanol, even outperforming traditional copper-based catalysts. Our findings highlight how localized electronic properties at the gold-cerium interface can be engineered to drive efficient $CO_2$ utilization.
humanized_abstract: Converting CO2 to fuel and chemicals is considered one of the strategies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. However, the high thermodynamic stability of CO2 molecules leads to large activation energy barriers, and so catalysts are needed. It has been shown that using nanoparticles of certain metals, particularly gold, on appropriate oxide supports can promote high catalytic activities. It was reported that the bulk gold metal was not catalytically active, while it could act as an active catalyst when it was used as nanoparticles supported on particular oxides. Using experiments and simulations based on density functional theory, the activity of Au nanoparticles supported on a mixed-oxide material of cerium and titanium for catalytic conversion of CO2 has been demonstrated. It has been revealed that Au nanoparticles anchored on CeO x/TiO2 were catalytically active and thereby facilitate the reduction of CO2 by hydrogen at relatively low pressures. A novel explanation is provided where a highly polarizing metal like Au induces an electronic polarization effect on a oxide material that in turn yields favorable adsorption sites for the CO2 molecules and catalytic activity toward reduction of CO2 into hydrocarbons at relatively low pressure, in particular, to form preferentially the desired product of methanol in greater yield than that of Cu catalysts.
